title: Safety Program/Risk Management: Security Personnel
description: Conroe ISD maintains security personnel to help keep students, staff, and visitors safe on school campuses and at school-related activities. The district outlines how these security staff are selected, trained, and deployed to protect the school community. Parents and community members can expect that safety measures involving security personnel follow established guidelines designed to create a secure learning environment.

timeline_local.summary: This is a new local policy (CKE) for Conroe ISD establishing the framework for Reserve Police Officers within the school district police department. The policy authorizes the chief of police to appoint reserve officers, defines their authority and qualifications per Education Code 37.0816, and clarifies that reserve officers are not eligible for full-time employment benefits or pension funds.
changes_added: The Board authorizes the chief of police of the school district police department to appoint reserve police officers for the district consistent with meeting safety needs and the availability of funds.
The District may provide an annual report to the Board on the appointment of reserve police.

A reserve police officer shall serve at the discretion of the chief of police of the school district police department and may be called into service at any time that the chief considers it necessary to have additional officers to preserve the peace and enforce the law.
The chief of police shall only authorize a reserve police officer who meets the qualifications outlined in Education Code 37.0816 and other relevant federal, state, and local laws and regulations.

The Chief of Police shall be responsible for ensuring that reserve police officers comply with all applicable requirements, duties, and responsibilities as set forth in Education Code 37.0816 and other relevant federal, state, and local laws and regulations.

A reserve police officer, regardless of whether the reserve police officer holds a permanent peace officer license issued under Chapter 1701, Occupations Code, is not eligible for participation in any program provided by the District or the Board that is normally considered a financial benefit of full-time employment or for any pension fund created by statute for the benefit of full-time paid peace officers and is not exempt from Chapter 1702, Occupations Code.
timeline_local.notes: This appears to be an entirely new policy (Version 1, no prior text exists), so all content is shown in red underline as additions. The cover page title heading is not a standard action phrase (it only shows the policy description and metadata without a "Discuss," "Adopt," or "Second Reading" prefix), so the board action could not be determined with confidence — marked as 'unknown'. The rationale states "Required by Legislative Change," suggesting this is driven by new legislation (likely related to Education Code 37.0816 on reserve police officers).

elements.element: Board authorization for chief of police to appoint reserve police officers
elements.statutory_basis: TEC §37.0816 as added by H.B. 1458 (89th Leg., R.S., 2025)
elements.supporting_quote: HB 1458 authorizes a school district's board of trustees to allow the chief of police of the school district police department to appoint reserve police officers for the district — web search confirmed permissive framing; no verbatim §37.0816 text retrieved in statute analysis
elements.local_additions: The policy adds the qualifier 'consistent with meeting safety needs and the availability of funds' — a local fiscal and operational gate not expressed in the statutory text. The board's choice to actually exercise this permissive authority rather than decline it is itself a locally discretionary act.

elements.element: Annual report to the Board on reserve police appointments
elements.local_additions: No statute in the analysis requires or expressly authorizes an annual report on reserve police appointments. This is a locally chosen accountability mechanism entirely within the district's discretion. The permissive policy language ('may provide') reinforces that this is a local choice.

elements.element: Reserve officer serves at chief's discretion and may be called into service to preserve the peace and enforce the law
elements.statutory_basis: TEC §37.0816 as added by H.B. 1458 (89th Leg., R.S., 2025)

This is consistent with, but not compelled by, the verified statutory framework.

elements.element: Qualification requirement — chief shall only authorize officers meeting TEC §37.0816 qualifications and other applicable law
elements.statutory_basis: TEC §37.0816 as added by H.B. 1458 (89th Leg., R.S., 2025); OC §1701.001(6) as amended by H.B. 1458
elements.supporting_quote: "Reserve law enforcement officer" means a person designated as a reserve law enforcement officer under Section 37.0816, Education Code, Section 85.004, 86.012, or 341.012, Local Government Code, or Section 60.0775, Water Code.
elements.verification_method: verbatim statute text (OC §1701.001(6)); web search confirmed for TEC §37.0816 enactment
elements.local_additions: The core §37.0816 qualification compliance is required — lawful designation as a reserve law enforcement officer flows only through §37.0816 per the verbatim OC §1701.001(6) quote. The policy extension to 'other relevant federal, state, and local laws and regulations' is a locally chosen drafting expansion beyond what the verified statutory text specifies, though compliance with other applicable law is a general legal obligation independent of this policy.

elements.element: Chief of Police responsible for ensuring ongoing compliance of reserve officers with §37.0816 requirements, duties, and responsibilities
elements.statutory_basis: TEC §37.0816 as added by H.B. 1458 (89th Leg., R.S., 2025)

elements.verification_method: web search confirmed — bill enacted; verbatim §37.0816 text not retrieved in statute analysis
elements.local_additions: Assigning the specific ongoing compliance oversight responsibility to the Chief of Police is a local structural and administrative choice. While §37.0816 defines the compliance framework, no verbatim statutory text in the analysis imposes this supervisory responsibility on the chief specifically. Under the Grounding Rule, this cannot be classified as Required by Law without a supporting verbatim quote.

elements.element: Reserve officer ineligibility for full-time employment financial benefits and pension funds

compliance_summary: Reserve Officer Program: Conroe ISD's new policy CKE creates a framework for reserve police officers within the district's police department, using authority provided by Texas Education Code §37.0816, added by H.B. 1458 during the 2025 legislative session.

Appointment Authority: The policy allows the chief of police to appoint reserve officers, consistent with safety needs and available funding. This reflects a local decision to use the option the state made available — the district was not required to create a reserve officer program.

Officer Qualifications: Reserve officers must meet the qualification standards set out in TEC §37.0816 and the Texas Occupations Code §1701.001(6). The policy also extends this requirement to other applicable federal, state, and local laws, which goes slightly beyond what the verified statutory text specifically requires.

Chief of Police Oversight: The policy assigns the chief of police responsibility for making sure reserve officers continue to meet all requirements under TEC §37.0816. This supervisory structure is a local administrative decision, as the statute does not specifically name the chief for this role.

Benefits Ineligibility: The policy states that reserve officers are not eligible for full-time employment benefits or pension funds. This appears to reflect a limitation included in TEC §37.0816, though the exact statutory wording was not available for confirmation.

Annual Reporting Option: The policy includes an option for the chief of police to report reserve officer appointments to the school board each year. No state law requires this — it is a locally chosen accountability measure the district added on its own.

timeline_local.summary: This policy update creates a brand-new local policy (CKE) for Conroe ISD governing Reserve Police Officers, establishing that the Board may authorize the chief of police to appoint reserve officers, defining their authority and discretionary service, setting qualification requirements under Education Code 37.0816, assigning compliance responsibility to the Chief of Police, and allowing the board to limit the number of reserve officers appointed.
changes_added: The Board may authorize the chief of police of the school district police department to appoint reserve police officers for the district.

A reserve police officer shall serve at the discretion of the chief of police of the school district police department and may be called into service at any time that the chief considers it necessary to have additional officers to preserve the peace and enforce the law.
The chief of police shall only authorize a reserve police officer who meets the qualifications outlined in Education Code 37.0816 and other relevant federal, state, and local laws and regulations.

The Chief of Police shall be responsible for ensuring that reserve police officers comply with all applicable requirements, duties, and responsibilities as set forth in Education Code 37.0816 and other relevant federal, state, and local laws and regulations.
The board may limit the number of reserve police officers that the chief may appoint.
timeline_local.notes: The document appears to be an entirely new policy (Version 1, all text shown in red underline), so all content is "added" with nothing removed. The cover page does not contain a standard action title heading (e.g., "Discuss," "Adopt," etc.) — it reads only as a policy header/label, so the board action could not be determined from the title alone and is marked 'unknown'. The document may be a first reading of a new policy.

description_local: This policy area covers a range of school operations, including how board members request information, how the district purchases goods and services, emergency planning, security staffing, and management of school buildings and grounds. It affects school board members, district staff, students, and the broader community. Parents and community members should be aware of these policies because they shape how safely and efficiently schools are run — from who is responsible for security on campus to how taxpayer dollars are spent on purchases and facilities.
